#36 Walking across Fukuoka to visit Ohori Park

May 15, 2024 1 hour 42 minutes

All transcripts are machine generated.

But I think that's also because the part I was at was at one point, a path of some kind. I think you would definitely not want to go down just at a random point because with the variableness of the terrain, it could fall off into a legit cliff at any moment. But yeah, there we go. We went down. Now we're heading back up. But yeah, it's one of these things that I just don't think will ever. Maybe if I legit lived here for like 20 years, it's just one of these lessons that I keep learning over and over, but I just don't think I'm ever going to realistically internalize the idea that you don't take the direct route. You don't go the quote unquote logical way. It's not going to work. You got to go the way someone else has determined. Okay, man, this Google translate nice. I've had another fork.

250 meters of this way to the deck. But yeah, as opposed to where I'm from in Canada, obviously if you live by the Rocky Mountains or something, you can't just, you know, there are impassable things, but there's still a lot less subtle than here. It's still like, bam, mountain range. Where I'm from in New Brunswick, you can just set a course, you know. There's a lot of nature, but it's a lot more sparse and everything is relatively flat. So, you know, you might have a bad time. There might be mosquitoes eating you to death or blackflies or bears trying to kill you.

And terrain-wise, you know, you might hit like a marsh. There's a lot of marsh land around there. So you can't for sure just pick a direction and walk, but basically you can. All the stuff that I keep trying to do here and constantly fail. Again, it's just because that's where I grew up, you can do that. You can be like, yeah, I don't want to take that route. Instead, why don't I just directly go from here to there? And it's going to be fine. Here in Japan, it's never fine. It never works. 40:00 And now I'm down in another valley. And this must be the final ascent. Another set of stairs going back up. I mean, I've gone up and down and up and down. So many times, it's like California Games, the BMX fucking track. This is again not a big park. You know, it was only 500 meters across to get to the other thing. So half a kilometer, I wasn't at the very edge, but this thing is less than a kilometer wide. And the amount of variation in the terrain is just out of control. It's fucking wild. It's so cool. It's so awesome. Lots of Joro spiders here. It's really big webs.

I feel much better about them when they're just a little bit off to the side.

It's hard to coexist when they're blocking the path. But as long as they're just off to the side, these gigantic terrifying spiders are actually very beautiful, very pretty.

Like, are you like fucking with me at this point? I met another fork, one that goes down and one that goes up. And the one that goes up goes directly to a fence once again. Yeah, I think I got to go down again, a hundred meters to go, and I got to go down one more time. It's so fucking weird.

Yeah, I guess it's of one of these things too. You would just internalize the terrain. If you grew up somewhere, this mountainous, you would just be used to this. You would expect it. Even if it was somewhere you'd never been before, you would presume that there's going to be a lot of ups and downs coming your way. Where I guess my version of that thing would be the marshlands and the swampy terrain. If you were just visiting New Brunswick, you might not expect that to happen. Where once I start feeling the bogginess underfoot, it's like, oh, I know what's happening. I know I can't keep going this way. It's not going to work. But yeah, I feel like I'm stepping on a rake constantly here. I just never, I keep trying to apply the terrain of my homeland to this terrain and it's just so incredibly different. This may be a little anticlimactic. I think I'm at the end. It's just like a big structure here. This must be the observation deck. It's an actual deck that you climb up. It does seem like that observation deck would be a little interesting because the, like, the Aburiyama observation deck that I've been to a bunch of times, it's from the mountain looking out at Fukuoka. Where this would be the reverse angle, I'm still just seeing, you know, a version of the view between the buildings and stuff as I walk down the hill. So that would be kind of cool. You know, like that shrine I went to even at the end of the night. I was like, you know, I just, I think I'll be okay. And I just walked the two hours home instead. It's like, I'm just not in the mood for squat toileting today. If it's a serious emergency, absolutely. But if I don't have to do it, maybe I am losing my, I never had a luster for squat toilets. They fucking suck. But there are definitely more squat toilets in Fukuoka, for sure. Now that I think about it, the one on the way up to the aburiyama observation deck is also a squat toilet. But today I was like, yeah, I'm going to treat myself today. No matter what this washroom is, I'm going to use it, even if it is a squat toilet. But it wasn't.

It was a beautiful Western toilet. But there was no toilet paper. And in fact, no placed even put toilet paper. So that's unusual. I have never seen that before where it does feel more like at a campground or something. But luckily, I have not needed my fabled technique that I had to use in Montreal, where when traveling socks equal toilet paper, I haven't had to repeat that yet.

A lot of times at 7-Eleven, depending on what you buy or convenience stores in general, they'll give you just little single use, little finger towel things. So I just kind of tuck those away. But I also have, I have some wet wipes that I brought with me like one of those little 15 packs that I brought from Canada. So it's actually getting a little bit dried out because I just haven't needed it. And I bought a backup pack from Family Mart that I haven't opened yet. So I've got my unopened emergency wet wipe pack. The cotton L one from Canada is just like slowly, slowly drying out. So I just left it there to try to be a good neighbor. Whoever the next person is who wanders in there, if they're like, geez, what's going on? No toilet paper. Ah, but some nice random stranger left me three quarters of a pack of wet wipes that are still mostly wet. Seems like the neighborly thing to do. And I was looking at my new favorite thing, these Bluetooth headphones that are not wireless. Like they're wired to each other. But then the entire thing is a Bluetooth. I like them fantastically more than the two individual earbuds. I mean right now at this very moment, I got one of my headphones is for this recorder. The other one is my little Bluetooth thingy. Just dangling from my ear because they're both connected. It's fucking genius. It's wonderful. I'm not quite sure why this isn't the more popular form function for these things. But generally these things are somewhat expensive. Certainly not three dollars. I found some at a thrift store for three dollars. So just as an experiment. I was like, I guess what I was thinking is, you know, when you go to like the dollar store and you can get headphones for a dollar and they're fucking shit. They're terrible. But technically they work. You know, if you really just needed some headphones, the little headphones that cost a dollar are usable. So I was like, I wonder, is there a dollar store version of Bluetooth headphones? It seems unlikely, but we're in the magical land of Japan. So who knows. I'm just walking by a fancy boutique machine. Cheelab. 50:00 The cheese short for cheese because it's camembert and others came on bears. The only one in English. Anyway, fancy cheeses. Looks like the most expensive one is eight dollars. No cheese for me though, thank you. But yeah, it's just so curious. There's no way these three dollar fucking headphones work, right? And they don't. It turns out they're just too old. There's some random, you know, Chinese brand of no particular affiliation and the batteries dead. When you plug them into the computer, they light up, like they're charging, but they just don't charge. But that's okay. It was worth it just for the experiment. Because I was also thinking, you know, Japan's very famous for its used items being in perfect condition. If you go by old used video games and stuff, they're gonna work. It's like no one ever owned them in the first place. And this technically also looked like no one had ever used it. It's just, it was just too old. It's the kind of thing I feel like hard off probably just wouldn't sell that item. But since this was just some random other secondhand shop, it's anyway. But it was worth three dollars just to experiment.

Man, you know, as you just wander randomly through Fukuoka as long as you're not just on the major streets, there are little parks everywhere. I guess again, because we're like the foothills of the mountain. So there's just, just bumps, just bumps in the world. And whenever a big bump happens, they're like, all right, put a park in there. Because yeah, I remember one time I went from Umabiyashi station when I was staying up there and just walked pretty much straight north to Fukuoka Tower. And on the way, I found a park like this where it's just a bump. You just go way, way up all of a sudden. And there's a cool view or whatever. And I thought like, oh wow, what a neat cool park that I found. And it was. But they're kind of all over the place. There's a bunch of them. That must be approximately noon. I always check the time when I hear a chime and it's not sinking in. No, it's 125. I guess that's the problem too. I don't mean they don't seem. I don't know. I just don't know what those. It's one of those things I never think to look up until, until it happens. But I don't have an internet connection out of the world. So who the heck knows, man? Anyway, when I was back home during COVID, and I would sit around dreaming, day dreaming, and night dreaming about Japan, it's like I should redo all the cool stuff that I did. So I was even thinking that, like, even though I'm not by Umabiyashi station anymore, it's like I could walk the hour or so over to Umabiyashi, then walk south and do the same walk as last time.

But now that I'm in Japan, I'm not often doing that type of thing where I just do the exact same route. Even yesterday, I went a different way than I've ever gone before. But this is definitely the best method because I'm still having the same approximate experience, but a little better. Because, you know, the second time's never as interesting. So this way, I get to throw new stuff into the mix. New versions of that random park that I found. But in this case, it feels a little weird because I deliberately sidestept the Fukuoka Botanical Gardens. And I mean, I'm sure it's pretty cool. I'm sure I would enjoy it to some degree. You know, it's six dollars. I could pay the six dollars. That's not a problem. I just always feel weird about doing tourism stuff because it, I don't know, it feels like I'm being put on the spot. Like, instead, I walked deliberately around the Botanical Gardens to Monami Park that's across the street. And I just expected, you know, when you see these things on the map, it's just a big green spot. I just expected it to be a normal blend of boring park. It's actually really weird where there's a little statue up here. But basically, you go up into the park and yeah, all the parks in Fukuoka are up mostly. And there's just this huge pit in the middle. So I'm like circling around the pit. And 55:00 it's just this dense jungle pit right in the middle. And this fucking cool is shit. But this is like a great example of I know the Botanical Gardens are beautiful. And it's nice that people develop these things. And I definitely don't don't begrudge them charging admission. You know, you got to keep these things going. Someone's got to pay to maintain these things.

But it's always just this feeling like I'm supposed to react. That's the feeling I don't like. You know, when I'm like presented with something and it's like, uh, now react. And not only react, but react affirmatively. You know, be impressed. Even if no one literally is there caring how I react, just even in my own mind, I'm supposed to care. And I don't like the feeling that I'm supposed to care. I just don't like it because I don't know. I feel like I just don't parse things that way. I feel like everything I see is cool and impressive on some level. Like this park is a perfect example. What the hell is this? What a weird park. Just a great big pit in the middle of a mountain. That's awesome. At the same time, it's just a park. It's just more Japan. It's just more Fukuoka. Like I, I think it's fucking awesome. And I just don't care at the same time. You know, it's just yet another experience. It's just one of the thousands. It's just the stream of consciousness happening around me. To put like a distinct pin on something, like you're supposed to care about this. Well, I don't. I do not. I don't care about anything in particular in life. I don't have any big peak that it's like, oh yeah, that's the thing that I care about. I just like it all generally. And this is one nice thing about traveling alone. Is I was just just in this moment. It just came back to me with the botanical gardens. Where I was thinking back. Way back to my first girlfriend that I had in Vancouver. And we did some traveling. And we had a great time. And we're still Facebook buddies and stuff. You know, we got along really well. And if we were traveling together, there is no question we would just go to the botanical gardens. And it would be fine. But it would not be what I really wanted to do. You know, that's what I'd have to put on. Just even if it's the most minor feeling of a performance. Of like, hey, we came all the way here. We paid admission. We walked around for an afternoon. Now react. How do you feel? And I don't feel anything. I don't give a shit. I don't give a shit about that park. I don't give a shit about this park. I don't give a shit about anything in that way. That's just not how I want to pretend that I feel about stuff. The way I feel about stuff is just it's so much more diffused. It doesn't matter what it is. Just throw it at me. Just let me walk through Japan. And I like it all.

And I don't know. I don't know if that makes sense. I don't know how many other people feel that way.

But again, I just hope that I hope the very nature of an idiosyncratic experience is interesting to people, even if the experience specifically is a bit different than what other people would do. I mean, obviously it's unusual to even have the 90-day visitor visa to just do whatever you want for three months. But I kind of suspect even if that was on the table a lot of people still wouldn't do what I do. They wouldn't just wander around at random. And you know what I feel like this is a perfect time because I meant to talk about it yesterday and I just got distracted with climbing a mountain. Is I had a little taste of the other type of travel, the kind of more conventional normal way to visit Japan? And I thought it was interesting. And it's also good to spend a little extra time in this park where it's shaded with trees because yeah, it is getting hotter and hotter out there. I really thought, like I thought I had a little worry in the back of my mind that coming in Japan in late October and staying here until January, I was like, you know, maybe this trip really won't be as good as the first one because the first one was early summer and just that pure blast of, you know, brain melting, like as uncomfortable as summer heat is, it is just kind of nice because it is like you're just in a sauna all day every day and it sort of boils your brain and it kind of 1:00:00 makes things seem a little more magical and dreamy than maybe they should then maybe they otherwise would. So I thought like, oh well, I'll come in the lower season, you know, the kind of skies overcast season. But man, I was wrong. It is not even close to that yet. I know that that season exists in Japan whenever the Japan vloggers and the YouTubers are doing stuff in winter, you're like, oh man, look how gray everything is. That's strange. But I certainly am not experiencing that. Holy shit. If anything, yeah, because I moved south here now and I'm in Fukuoka, it is ludicrous. It was already like summer in Tokyo and now it's like double summer. It's crazy. Oh and also, I guess I'll toss it in just a quick mention with the botanical gardens is a zoo. I presume the zoo is more expensive. I'd spend six dollars to go to a zoo. But I didn't I didn't look into it. Zoo's are one of those things. It's very much the same as how I feel about vegetarianism. I'm not a vegetarian, but if the hammer came down and for some reason everyone had to be vegetarian, I always think of like aliens come to earth and tell us to stop killing things. I would have no problem with it at all. I'd be like, you know what? Good idea. We probably should have been doing this all along. No problem. And I feel the same way about zoos. I can see why zoos could be a bad thing. If suddenly everyone decided we got outlawed zoos, that'd be fine. But I just I just don't feel passionately about it. I don't know. I started reading life of pi once and I did not get very far. But the early chapters are about running a zoo and that guy kind of made some pitches for why zoos aren't so bad. I mean, it just depends on the zoo. There's certainly two sides to the story. I haven't been to very many zoos in my life, but I mean, they are interesting just to see stuff like I saw giraffes in Ontario. And when you see them in person, it's like, damn, they're just so much bigger than I realized. They're fucking huge. So I mean, that's an interesting thing because you just it doesn't sink in through TV or through a book like it does when this thing is right in front of you. And it's like, holy fuck. It's quite a scary animal in fact.

But yeah, so the second day I was at Norahiko's Airbnb in Chiba. When he took me to the Ezekiah and I met some of his friends and we got all crazy drunk. So we were back at the Airbnb and that's when I switched over from the four drinks I had at the Ezekiah, which were already too many but would have been manageable. Old Norahiko brought out some whiskey and me and his friends at Toru fucking drank the whole goddamn thing and we were both fucked up. We both threw up. I messaged him on Facebook before I left just saying like, hey, fairly well my friend. You know, I also had a, because he passed out first and I was like, yeah, me too dude. And he said, yeah, Jesus, I regretted that the next day. The washroom here, I've never seen this before, where the men and women bathroom signs in Japan. It's always just a little cartoon man and a cartoon lady and they're in blue and pink, which I was, I mean, that's stood out to me a bit in 2019 also. I mean, it is legitimately handy from a distance just to tell which ones which. But I was like, I bet that's the kind of thing in North America we already would frown upon. Like gender specific colors for the washroom. No, thank you. But it is kind of handy. It's useful. Anyway, here, the little man was in a tux or a little fancy suit with a bow tie and the lady one was like, she looked like a little ballerina, you know, like from a music box.

But it's not so fancy inside. There's a squat toilet. It's like, hmm, it's kind of neat though.

Fuck, what was I just saying? Oh, yeah, okay. So, yeah, we were super drunk back at the Airbnb as the new people came in. So that was Anna and her brother from Columbia. So that first night, we just hung out and talked and whatever. And I thought I had my drunkenness under control, you know, like I was saying in that podcast about it, that at least I didn't have one of those things where you wake up the next day and like a gong hits you and you realize all the embarrassing things you said. I was like, oh, nothing like that happened. So I think I handled stuff all right, despite the fact that yeah, it was crazy sick and had to crawl to the bathroom and threw up and it was just miserable. But yeah, we made fast friends, became Facebook friends, yadda yadda. But then I didn't see them again. They were staying the exact same number of days as me. We were both leaving on the same day. Oh, this path is leading me now down into the pit, into this pit in the center of Minami Park. There's a more gradual path that someone else is coming up. So I'm going to take these stone stairs straight down. They, our schedules just did not line up and it turns out that is because they are super travelers. I mean, they were only in Japan for 10 days, you know, again, much more the common situation than what I do. They were in Chiba for five days. And basically they left in the morning and they didn't come back till way late at night. They were gone all day. But then on the last night, it all worked out. They came back early because they had to get up at 5.30 in the morning. The next day to head out. So we got to hang out non-drunkingly, although Norahiko, he's too good of a host. I mean, I told him how sick I was. But he brought out the whiskey again, made him, made us each a drink. But then he just took his upstairs to go hang out with his wife. So I was sipping at the one he made for me, but I just knew if it's just sitting in front of me, even if I'm just sipping while I talk to Anna and her brother. Eventually, I'm in a drink. So I poured it out. I was like, I'm sorry, Norahiko. I can't do it. I can't do it. Boos is the devil.

I'm more than happy enough to just soak in whatever's happening around me in Japan. At this point, I don't need to be traveling super far away, necessarily. But I'm not like against it. It's like, okay, that's, but that also explains why I never saw them. They had to leave in the morning, come back late, to get to these places, eats up a lot of the day. But then they showed me kind of the whole map of where they'd been in the past 10 days. And yeah, it's like crazy. How I've been here a whole month, and I've kind 1:10:00 of gone nowhere, you know, just within Tokyo. And just like, first time near Shinjuku, then I'm out west, then I'm in Chiba. Then it's like, whoa, Fukuoka, like, okay, that's a bit of a change, but even once I'm here, I'm still just walking around Fukuoka. Yeah, this does seem to be a dead end.

Where they started, well, actually, I can tell you, because this is a cool thing, is they had little spots saved on Google maps of all the places they'd been. So I was like, hey, I should make a marking just in case I, you know, probably not for this trip, but maybe next time. If next time I come to Japan, if I try to stretch things out more, let me mark Nico on my map. And she was like, hey, wait a sec, I can share with you the list I made of all the places we went, which I had no idea that was an option in Google Maps. So yeah, I've got 49 places edited five days ago of all the places they went. So roughly speaking, they started in Yamaguchi prefecture, which is on Honshu, it is on the main island, but it's not that far from where I am now in the sense of all of Japan. I guess kind of near Hiroshima, they went to a bunch of places there. They hopped over near Kobe to this island beneath Kobe. Then a whole bunch of places more near Osaka up into the Kyoto region a bit more. Over to Nagoya. So a lot of these are this sort of more known places, but then, okay, this one must be because it's near Mount Fuji. Yamaguchi, I didn't recognize that, that Mount Fuji is really nearby. Then a fuck ton of stuff in Tokyo and over to Chiba. Oh, and they went east of Chiba too. What is over here? What is this? Oh, that's Nareda. But anyway, the amount of stuff they did in 10 days is fucking crazy. Like, that's kind of interesting in its own way. That's like very different than what I do. But man, like I feel like I pack in a lot of experiences just walking around doing nothing. Like, I wasn't sure before I started this podcast, like, will there be stuff to say? Will I be able to do a podcast every day? How is that possible? Surely it'll just, you know, I'll just be walking around. But it's no problem if anything, pretty much every day. I feel like I'm putting things off stuff that it's like, oh, I should mention this, I should mention that. And I don't get around to it. I feel like every day that I'm in Japan is just something interesting. But they did this, you know, like times 30. And just like, boom, soak it in, get all the experiences, go all the places, see all the stuff. And the magic, the magic explanation for how this is possible that hadn't really crossed my mind, it's something I was aware of, but I never looked into it, is the JR rail pass. And this is something I really should look into. I don't actually have the details of how it works. You will have to do your own research. But basically, it's like a, you can't take just like literally every train, but it's a special pass you get that you can take a lot of the trains. And there's like a special discount if you are a visitor. But you got to arrange it ahead of time. Before you get to Japan, you set up to pay for the rail pass, and then you pick it up when you get here. And I don't know how many days it is, but I think it is more along the lines of what they did, like for a 10 day thing, no problem. I don't think I can get a 90 day rail pass, you know, it's a whole different type of visiting at that point. But it's just such a neat idea because that's how they hit all these places. You know, they went almost as far as I went to Fukuoka, but they were a bit surprised to hear that I was flying within Japan. And I was surprised to hear that they weren't. As a barrier, it's like these diagonal barriers so that people can sit there and they just sit on the sides of the 1:35:00 steps.
